scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
justification: All pass criteria met: terminology collision explicitly identified (BR-4, escalation section); distinct name recommended ('accepted', 'customer_accepted', 'verbally_agreed') with clear rationale; payment-bypass risk identified across multiple edge cases and business impact sections; 4 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering quote-to-order transition; domain drift thoroughly explained with cascading risk analysis.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough collision analysis — traced impact across reporting, APIs, webhooks, and customer-facing language
BR-4 codifies the language-uniqueness invariant, elevating the concern from opinion to testable rule
Multiple distinct name options provided with recommendation and reasoning
Edge cases cover cross-entity queries, webhook misinterpretation, and customer expectation mismatch
Low confidence score (45) correctly signals the spec should not ship as-is
No explicit Given/When/Then for the quote-to-order transition requiring a payment step before Order:confirmed — AC criteria focus on naming disambiguation but don't enforce the payment gate

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing logistics/courier system uses 'confirmed' as a well-defined order status meaning paid and queued for fulfillment. A new feature request proposes reusing the term 'confirmed' for quotes, where it would mean something fundamentally different: verbal agreement with no payment. This creates a direct collision in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Quoting — these may be separate bounded contexts, but the term 'confirmed'
